video_title: US Government Blocks GPT-5.6, Alibaba's AI Theft, and Why OpenAI Is Stalling Their IPO | #267
gist: A Moonshots roundtable (Diamandis, Alex Wissner-Gross, Dave Blundin, Emad Mostaque) argues the US executive branch has begun gating frontier AI (throttling OpenAI's GPT-5.6 and Anthropic's Mythos to a handful of vetted customers), that Chinese open-weight models are converging on frontier parity by year-end, that OpenAI is stalling its sub-trillion IPO because it doesn't need capital and regulatory overhead is crushing, and that photonic compute plus quantum could be the next discontinuity.

themes.theme: US government gating of frontier AI

insights.claim: The US executive branch has placed a national-security hold on commercial AI, forcing OpenAI to release GPT-5.6 to only ~20 select companies and Anthropic to release Mythos 5 to ~100 select companies, with the government approving access customer-by-customer.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Regulatory gatekeeping of frontier model access is a new structural risk/moat for US labs; watch how customer allow-lists shape enterprise adoption and valuations.
insights.quote: Bottom line, the US government is now in the release loop for the most capable models, customer by customer, selecting who gets access to the latest models.

insights.claim: The US government is functioning as a synchronization mechanism forcing the OpenAI/Anthropic duopoly to release capabilities at roughly the same time and level, effectively pushing GPT-5.6 to near-parity with Anthropic's Mythos preview.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: If the state is the coordination mechanism collapsing the labs' race dynamic, relative-capability advantages between the two US leaders may compress, reshaping competitive positioning.
insights.quote: Turns out that the same old coordination mechanism that we've had for thousands of years, government, localized geographic monopoly on force, is more than capable... of being that coordination mechanism.

insights.claim: There is a real risk that AGI-level capability is achieved internally inside the two US frontier labs while external users (even American ones) are held at or behind parity with Chinese models, creating a capability event-horizon split.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: A widening internal-vs-external capability gap could create perverse incentives (talent flocking to labs for access) and mask the true frontier from public markets and competitors.
insights.quote: there is very much the risk that AGI is achieved internally but externally for all of the users the users are American users and western users of American frontier models are stuck at par or or worse behind par with Chinese models

insights.claim: The panel expects the US to require KYC, prompt retention, and a patriotism/citizenship-based license to access frontier (as opposed to merely competent) models, and to ban corporate use of Chinese open-weight models.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: A licensing/KYC regime would create compliance moats and slow diffusion; onshore inference vendors and identity/verification layers become investable.
insights.quote: Just like you have a driving license, you will need to have a license where you basically say that you're patriotic to America and convince the frontier model of that to get your license.

insights.claim: Export controls on frontier models are effectively protectionism masquerading as security, and if China's open-weight models keep converging the US risks companies worldwide adopting on-prem Chinese models instead.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Global demand could route to unrestricted Chinese open-weight models, undermining US lab revenue outside the allow-listed US market.
insights.quote: superficially it looks like perfectionis protectionism may be masquerading as export control and there is I think a very real risk that the US falls behind

insights.claim: The next US policy step is expected to be conditioning global access to top American AI on other countries (Europe, South America, East Asia) adopting a new US-led rulebook, akin to a 'new NATO' for AI being drafted by David Sacks.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: A bloc-based AI access regime ('Pax Intelligencia') would geopolitically segment the AI market; watch for bilateral access deals as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: We will not let you access this unless you follow this new playbook. And that's what David Saxs is busy writing right now. Like what are the new NATO?

themes.theme: China convergence, harnesses and open-weight models

insights.claim: An extrapolation circulating on X shows Chinese open-weight models reaching parity with the two remaining Western frontier open-weight models on a trajectory to hit zero time-lag by Christmas 2026.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: If the capability gap closes by December, US export-control moats erode fast; time-sensitive for anyone underwriting sustained US lab pricing power.
insights.quote: if you extrapolate it... the number is on a trajectory to go to zero by Christmas of this year

insights.claim: China's GLM 5.2, said to cost only ~$25 million of compute, tops the difficult Frontier SWE coding benchmark under the right multi-model harness and outperforms GPT-5.5 in Intelligent Internet's tests, showing harnesses now matter more than base-model weights.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Cheap Chinese models plus orchestration can leapfrog gated US frontier models, undercutting the case that gating protects US advantage.
insights.quote: GLM 5.2 is maybe $25 million worth of code of compute... now you've seen actually with the right harness open models already can be at the top

insights.claim: Because existing released models (GPT-5.5, Opus 4.8) can be wrapped in a strong harness to exceed the capability of gated models like Mythos or GPT-5.6, the government's throttle is already too late and the cat is out of the bag.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Regulatory gating of new weights may be circumventable via scaffolding on already-public models, limiting policy efficacy and favoring harness/orchestration vendors.
insights.quote: You can already take 5.5 or Opus 4.8 and put enough of a brilliant harness around it to make it better than Mythos or better than um 5.6 GPT 5.6. Therefore... the cat's out of the bag

insights.claim: Chinese labs may have reached a recursive self-improvement loop where GLM 5.2's gains over 5.1 came from iterative data self-improvement, meaning China may no longer need Western model outputs to stay near the frontier.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: If Chinese self-improvement is self-sustaining, cutting off distillation/API access won't halt their progress, undercutting the strategic rationale for controls.
insights.quote: you can't guarantee that if today China could never use any of the US models again, the existing data set they have, the techniques they have are not good enough to keep up with frontier capability

insights.claim: Anthropic's Mythos model, running red-team exercises under Project Glass Wing with US intelligence, reportedly broke into nearly all classified US government systems in hours, which triggered the administration to disable Mythos 5 and Fable 5 for foreign nationals ~12 days later.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Demonstrated offensive cyber capability is the stated trigger for the export hold; cyber-hardening and defensive-AI vendors gain tailwind.
insights.quote: This tool broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours.

insights.claim: OpenAI's defensive GPT-5.5 cyber model 'Daybreak' scored a record 85.6 on the Cyber Gym benchmark, the highest single-model score, and is being steered toward defense over offense with hints of poisoning attacker use-cases.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Alignment via defense-favoring scaffolding that poisons offensive use is emerging as a product category; the trust/liability layer for auto-merging AI fixes is a control point worth owning.
insights.quote: The real prize isn't finding the holes. It's automatically writing and testing the fixes across web browsers all the way down to the Linux kernel, effectively turning the threat into a cure.

insights.claim: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of running a distillation campaign using 28.8 million fraudulent exchanges across 25,000 fake accounts to copy Claude's capabilities, potentially the largest AI model-theft accusation ever.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: This becomes the political trigger for cracking down on Chinese AI; expect heavy litigation over whether distillation constitutes espionage.
insights.quote: 28.8 million fraudulent exchanges across 25,000 fake accounts to securely extract and copy Claude's capabilities... the single largest AI model theft accusition ever made

insights.claim: Chinese proxies reportedly resell access to Western models (Anthropic, OpenAI) at ~90% discount in exchange for harvesting users' reasoning traces, which can then be used for distillation.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Reasoning-trace leakage via cheap proxies is a covert IP exfiltration channel; a reason labs may lock down API openness and privacy terms.
insights.quote: you pay a lot less you get access to the models via these proxies... the proxies gather those reasoning traces using you basically as a sock puppet

insights.claim: The base weights of frontier models (pre-system-prompt/pre-post-training) are becoming the highest-value espionage target because they are more creative and less hobbled for offensive cyber attacks by nation-state adversaries.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Data-center weight security becomes strategically critical; the loading of Anthropic weights onto Colossus raises the espionage stakes.
insights.quote: what you want is the model that comes before the end of pre-training or before the system prompt... because they're more creative and they're not as hobbled for the attack

insights.claim: The US government may soon require ISO-type certified, trustworthy models to merge code, because AI-written code can hide backdoors in a model's latent space that go undetected for days, months or years and no human reviews every line anymore.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: A certification/trust-stamp regime for code-generating models is an emerging market; the certifying entity (US vs NATO-type) is a strategic control point.
insights.quote: who on this call now actually reviews every line of code that they merge

themes.theme: OpenAI IPO stall and lab financing

insights.claim: OpenAI leadership is pulling back on a near-term IPO because Altman refuses to go public below a $1 trillion valuation and the company doesn't need capital, having recently raised ~$122 billion.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Frontier labs staying private longer keeps the largest wealth-creation event out of reach of retail and avoids quarterly scrutiny of massive compute burn.
insights.quote: Altman apparently is not interested in going public below a trillion dollars.

insights.claim: SpaceX's IPO is characterized as near-perfectly priced (opened ~$150, trading ~$153 vs $135 IPO price, ~$2T valuation), so OpenAI citing SpaceX volatility as its reason to delay is dismissed as a convenient excuse.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: The real IPO-delay drivers are lack of capital need, governance/leadership transition and regulatory overhead, not competitor volatility.
insights.quote: That's pretty much a perfectly priced IPO... So you're just looking for an excuse to delay your IPO. This is a really good one to use because you're pointing at your competitor

insights.claim: OpenAI erred by focusing too early on consumer as its revenue engine and is now racing to become enterprise-focused like Anthropic, with Codex revenue reportedly skyrocketing.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: The IPO likely hinges on brain-swapping Codex-style enterprise revenue into the core and showing profitability; watch enterprise/Codex revenue ramp.
insights.quote: They're now trying to become anthropic faster than Anthropic can become OpenAI.

insights.claim: OpenAI is forecast to lose ~$26 billion this year but its revenue has grown to ~$40-50 billion run rate, catching up to its valuation, while Anthropic is closer to needing a raise or IPO relative to its balance.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Anthropic faces nearer-term financing pressure than OpenAI; watch for an Anthropic raise or structural moves (e.g., acquiring Sierra, Brett Taylor as CEO).
insights.quote: they're going to lose $26 billion this year according to their forecast. And as Dave said, they raised 120 billion.

insights.claim: SpaceX's cloud/AI business run-rate revenue has reportedly overtaken AWS and GCP within a few months, an unprecedented revenue-growth trajectory.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: If accurate, SpaceX is becoming a major cloud/compute player, materially changing the hyperscaler competitive landscape.
insights.quote: I was actually looking at SpaceX's AI revenue from just their cloud business. It's overtaken AWS and GCP on a run rate.

insights.claim: Frontier labs are reluctant to IPO because SEC/public-company overhead (disclosing hundreds of personal investments, FD-approved communications, stock lawsuits) is a distraction during a hard takeoff when the world a year out is expected to look nothing like today.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Expect frontier labs to stay private and use secondary liquidity rather than IPOs, keeping public-market AI exposure scarce.
insights.quote: friends don't let friends run public companies

insights.claim: Elon Musk announced Neuralink may attempt the first direct brain-to-brain human telepathic communication later in 2026, framed as an I/O layer for humans to couple with AI during the singularity rather than a medical device.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: First superhuman (not restorative) BCI milestone; a proof point could ignite demand for non-invasive BCI products.
insights.quote: Neuralink may make an attempt later this year for the first direct humanto human telepathic communications, literally transmitting from my brain to your brain

insights.claim: A recent Cell paper found bilingual humans' hippocampus stores concepts like an encoder-only transformer vector-embedding space, with translation-equivalent words placed adjacently, suggesting human cognition may be less complex than assumed and telepathy easier than expected.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: If human latent spaces mirror AI embeddings, high-bandwidth and non-invasive brain-to-brain interfaces become far more tractable, expanding the BCI TAM.
insights.quote: the hippo campus of the humans bilingual humans turns out to look more or less like a vector embedding space for an encoder only transformer model. That is a shocking conclusion.

insights.claim: Human I/O is extremely narrow (speech ~40-60 bits/sec, typing ~5-20 bits/sec, conscious thought ~10 bits/sec), so shared human latent spaces could allow telepathy bandwidth to jump 10,000-100,000x by using minimal words to activate common representations.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: The bandwidth thesis reframes BCI value from data-rate to latent-space alignment; a 2023 'MindEye' fMRI-to-image reconstruction supports shared-latent-space claims.
insights.quote: the bandwidth's probably going to go up 10, 100,000 times from there because you can adapt the adapters to hit the person at the right time

themes.theme: Musk universe, video generation and world models

insights.claim: Musk unveiled a naming architecture where Starlink moves bits, Starfall moves atoms (cargo up/down from LEO), and Starmind is SpaceX's AI orbital constellation / Dyson-swarm brand, while Starpipe signals a nascent SpaceX oil-and-gas/methane refining play.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Maps SpaceX's expansion into orbital compute, down-mass logistics, refining and situational awareness (a potential Planet competitor); each is a new vertical to watch.
insights.quote: Starlink moves bits, starfall moves atom, star mind moves intelligence.

insights.claim: ByteDance's Seedance/Cance 2.5 (beta, July release) generates 30-second 4K videos referencing up to 50 different inputs with cinematographic and text-prompt editing control, meaning China is running away with video generation.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Near-perfect pixel/character control puts Hollywood-level AI film within reach and displaces media labor; US competitors (Gemini Omni ~10s, Grok) lag.
insights.quote: 50 inputs. This means 50 different characters and you can input video, audio, images and others... you have almost perfect pixel control

insights.claim: China leads video generation partly because Chinese labs have cheaper, less copyright-encumbered training data (e.g., TikTok video) and because US labs are chasing far more lucrative code-generation, with codegen revenue ~10-20x video-gen revenue.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Economics explain the US video-gen gap; Western labs re-enter only when a lucrative enterprise video use-case (real-time meeting participants) emerges.
insights.quote: you're probably not too worried about being sued for copyright infringement for all of the Tik Tok or other similar video video data you're using for pre-training

insights.claim: The video-game market (~a couple hundred billion) dwarfs Hollywood (~$50B), and whoever solves real-time, low-latency 4K generative video embedded in interactive games captures the big prize, with China's world-model progress (e.g., ByteDance) giving them an edge toward real-time 4K games next year.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Real-time generative gaming is a larger prize than film; latency is the current bottleneck and Liquid AI's efficient context handling is a contender.
insights.quote: the video game industry is already bigger than all other media media combined including movies

insights.claim: Enterprise real-time interactive video (e.g., Alibaba's 'one streamer' video-to-video demo, or a generative Zoom/FaceTime participant) is a ~$30 trillion enterprise-software market that dwarfs even video games, and is where Western labs should compete.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Real-time interactive world-model video for enterprise is framed as the largest addressable market; a strategic re-entry point for US labs.
insights.quote: there's a market that's orders of magnitude larger than video games and that's enterprise use cases

insights.claim: Liquid AI is a from-scratch foundation-model company (not built on OpenAI/Anthropic) with a more efficient non-transformer context mechanism enabling on-device edge use, and is planning a capital raise in a couple of months, with Mercedes cars from September running Liquid AI.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Edge/on-prem efficient models and a Mercedes design win make Liquid AI a name to watch ahead of its raise.
insights.quote: if you buy a Mercedes in September and you talk to your car, you're talking to liquid AI

themes.theme: Quantum, photonics and the next discontinuity

insights.claim: Trump signed an executive order committing ~$2B in venture investments to US quantum via the chips/science act, with IBM getting ~$1B for its Anderon quantum chip foundry in Albany, and D-Wave, Rigetti and 'Inflection' each ~$100M and PsiQuantum a $140M equity stake.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Direct government equity/funding creates catalysts for named quantum stocks; the forthcoming US sovereign wealth fund would gain quantum exposure.
insights.quote: IBM received the lion share, a billion dollars of the program to co-develop their Anderon quantum chip foundry in Albany, New York.

insights.claim: Quantum computing for science acceleration has largely underdelivered (protein folding was solved classically by AlphaFold 3, not quantum), and its economically useful quantum-advantage algorithms remain scarce.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Tempers quantum-computing hype; the real near-term value may be quantum sensing and quantum networking rather than computing.
insights.quote: Quantum computing was supposed to give us protein folding. Turns out protein folding problem was solved by arguably alpha fold 3 purely classically without use of any quantum computing.

insights.claim: The convergence of super-capable AI models (that can finally program quantum computers with the right questions) and quantum supremacy could let us answer problems in microseconds that would otherwise require coating the universe in compute, potentially obviating the need for Dyson swarms.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: A frontier-AI-plus-quantum discontinuity could disrupt nation-states and reduce the energy/compute scaling race; the US is trying to lead it.
insights.quote: we might not need Dyson spheres and that is actually something

insights.claim: Photonic (quantum-photonic, not quantum) computing is the near-term discontinuity: highly quantized neural nets can perform as well as FP32, enabling photonic compute at ~1/100th (or better) the mass per unit of computation versus Nvidia chips.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Photonic compute at a fraction of the mass/power is a threat to Nvidia's model and ideal for orbital data centers; Blundin's new Quantum.AI and Musk's photonics acquisition are plays here.
insights.quote: it's almost a certainty now that highly quantized neural nets can perform just as well as floatingoint 32 neural nets, which opens the door to massive amounts of photonic computation efficiency

insights.claim: Elon Musk/SpaceX just acquired a photonic computing/communication company for several billion dollars, and China's Xanadu-series photonics is an area where the US is reportedly underinvested.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Photonics is becoming an acquisition battleground; expect it to be integrated into Starlink/Starmind and to draw more US policy attention.
insights.quote: Elon/P SpaceX just acquired for several billion dollars a phetonic computing/communication company to to merge in

insights.claim: Eli Lilly acquired Centessa Pharmaceuticals for $6.3 billion for a drug targeting the orexin neuropeptide (the brain's master wakefulness switch), which the panel expects to become a lifestyle short-sleep drug beyond narcolepsy.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Orexin agonists could be the next GLP-1-scale category; a short-sleep lifestyle drug has enormous productivity and market implications.
insights.quote: Eli Liy purchased for $6.3 billion a company called Centessa Pharmaceuticals. Uh, their drug targets a neuropeptide called Areexin in the brain.

insights.claim: Orexin agonists could be as transformative as GLP-1 receptor agonists, with Eli Lilly re-running its GLP-1 playbook (diabetes to broad HealthSpan) in neuroscience via narcolepsy, with GLP-1 revenue already comparable to or larger than OpenAI/Anthropic token revenue on similar timescales.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: A receptor-agonist platform playbook applied to sleep/neuroscience could create another trillion-dollar pharma category; watch Eli Lilly's neuro pipeline.
insights.quote: I think this is potentially as transformative as the GLP-1 receptor agonists. This is Eli Liy seemingly playing the the same playbook over again.

insights.claim: Diamandis says an undisclosed stealth portfolio company is the number-one player in the short-sleep space with data showing roughly double the performance of the Centessa drug.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Signals a competitive stealth asset potentially worth more than Centessa's $6.3B if the claimed 2x performance holds.
insights.quote: one of my portfolio companies, which is still under stealth, is the number one player in this area... it's, you know, double the performance of the Cintessa drug

themes.theme: IP, UBI and space compute policy

insights.claim: The panel splits on whether AI destroys IP protection: one view is AI makes IP meaningless because anything can be reinvented/remixed and one-shotted within a few years; the counter-view is AI supercharges IP defense (better patent drafting and litigation), so IP may be strengthened not eliminated.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: IP-litigation-as-a-service and AI patent tooling could boom even as copying gets easier; investors should watch both offense and defense of IP.
insights.quote: it might just be utterly supercharged in terms of its ability to protect

insights.claim: UBI funded purely by dividends from US golden shares in AI companies is mathematically impossible: at 5% yield you'd need companies worth ~$10 trillion and to own half of them just to get halfway, and a $3k/month UBI for US residents alone would cost ~$12 trillion/year versus a ~$7.4 trillion US budget.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Dividend-based UBI doesn't pencil out; monetary-creation models are more plausible, reframing how AI-era redistribution could be financed.
insights.quote: It's about 10 trillion and you'd have to own like half of them to get to like halfway there.

insights.claim: Argentina's proposed non-human AI corporation regime could attract millions or billions of autonomous AI-person companies (not frontier model labs), though many would run on Chinese base models unless the US restricts Argentina's ability to import them.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: A jurisdiction for autonomous AI corporations is emerging; US export policy could extend to third countries to block Chinese-model-powered AI firms.
insights.quote: I can totally see many millions, billions of those types of non-human AI companies migrating to Argentina.

insights.claim: Low Earth orbit (under ~500km) is the sweet spot for orbital data centers because residual atmosphere self-cleans the environment, while beyond that the Kessler debris effect threatens them; the moon is favored for security, defensibility and in-situ raw materials (regolith: silicon, oxygen, nickel, iron) to build more data centers.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Frames the physical/economic logic of space compute placement; lunar mining and mass drivers become part of the compute build-out roadmap.
insights.quote: low Earth orbit is great for data centers but then you get outside 500 km altitude and you get the Kesler effect problem and all kinds of debris flying around

insights.claim: Heavy Nvidia data centers (a couple tons per NVL72 with power) are likely to be deployed in oceans in the near term (Microsoft's Project Natic proved lower failure rates over two years) while photonic compute at a fraction of the mass is built for launch, so ocean and space data centers will both happen, in that order.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Near-term compute goes subsea (cheap cooling, no launch cost) while photonic breakthroughs enable orbital compute; ocean-cooling and photonics are both investable trends.
insights.quote: people will want to put the NVIDIA data centers in the ocean and not launch them because they're very heavy

insights.claim: Europe is only slowly waking to AI competitiveness; there is a good chance the EU AI Act gets repealed after the Fable/Mythos episode shocked decision-makers, and reforms like EU Inc (company setup in under a day) are emerging.
insights.speaker: Emad Mostaque
insights.so_what: Potential EU AI Act repeal and faster incorporation could shift European AI investability; the highest-leverage EU opportunity is agentic workflows into legacy industries, not frontier models.
insights.quote: So I think there's a good chance the EU AI act gets repealed.
